title: Irish Congress of Trade Unions

infobox.value: Phil Ní Sheaghdha, President [Owen Reidy](/wiki/Owen_Reidy "Owen Reidy"), General Secretary

(often abbreviated to just **Congress** or **ICTU**), formed in 1959 by the merger of the [Irish Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Irish_Trades_Union_Congress "Irish Trades Union Congress") (founded in 1894) and the [Congress of Irish Unions](/wiki/Congress_of_Irish_Unions "Congress of Irish Unions") (founded in 1945), is a [national trade union centre](/wiki/National_trade_union_centre "National trade union centre"), the [umbrella organisation](/wiki/Umbrella_organization "Umbrella organization") to which trade unions in both the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland affiliate.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Umbrella organization for trade unions

Congress

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Irish Congress of Trade Unions | |
| Comhdháil na gCeardchumann | |
|  | |
| Founded | 1959 |
| Headquarters | [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), Ireland |
| Location | * Ireland (Republic of Ireland, Northern Ireland) |
| Members | * 602,000 (ROI) * 230,000 (NI) |
| Key people | Phil Ní Sheaghdha, President [Owen Reidy](/wiki/Owen_Reidy "Owen Reidy"), General Secretary |
| Affiliations | [ITUC](/wiki/International_Trade_Union_Confederation "International Trade Union Confederation"), [ETUC](/wiki/ETUC "ETUC"), [TUAC](/wiki/TUAC "TUAC") |
| Website | [Republic](http://www.ictu.ie) [Northern Ireland](https://www.ictuni.org) |

The **Irish Congress of Trade Unions** (often abbreviated to just **Congress** or **ICTU**), formed in 1959 by the merger of the [Irish Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Irish_Trades_Union_Congress "Irish Trades Union Congress") (founded in 1894) and the [Congress of Irish Unions](/wiki/Congress_of_Irish_Unions "Congress of Irish Unions") (founded in 1945), is a [national trade union centre](/wiki/National_trade_union_centre "National trade union centre"), the [umbrella organisation](/wiki/Umbrella_organization "Umbrella organization") to which trade unions in both the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland affiliate.

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Influence")]

There are currently 55 trade unions with membership of Congress, representing about 600,000 members in the Republic of Ireland.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Trade union members represent 35.1% of the Republic's workforce.[[2]](#cite_note-2) This is a significant decline since the 55.3% recorded in 1980 and the 38.5% reported in 2003.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In the Republic, roughly 50% of union members are in the public sector. The ICTU represents trade unions in negotiations with employers and the government with regard to pay and working conditions

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure")]

The supreme policy-making body of Congress is the Biennial Delegate Conference, to which affiliated unions send delegates. On a day-to-day basis Congress is run by an executive committee and a staffed secretariat headed up by the general secretary, Owen Reidy who succeeded Patricia King in the position in 2022.

Phil Ní Sheaghdha of the Irish Nurses and Midwives Organisation (INMO) became President of Congress at the biennial conference in Belfast in July 2025 succeeding Justin McCamphill of the NASUWT. The president serves for a two-year period and is normally succeeded by one of two vice-presidents.

Congress is the sole Irish affiliate of the [ETUC](/wiki/European_Trade_Union_Confederation "European Trade Union Confederation"), the representative body for trade unions at European level and of the International Trade Union Confederation [ITUC](/wiki/ITUC "ITUC")

## Social pacts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Social pacts")]

Congress enjoyed unprecedented political and economic influence over the period from 1987 to 2009 under the umbrella of Ireland's [social partnership](/wiki/Social_partnership "Social partnership") arrangements[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. This involved a series of seven [corporatist](/wiki/Corporatist "Corporatist") agreements with the government and the main manufacturing/services employer body [IBEC](/wiki/Ibec "Ibec") and the construction employers' lobby, CIF (Construction Industry Federation). It was a classic European-style alliance of government, labour and capital built on six decades of voluntary employment relations regulated by state institutions such as the Labour Court.

For many years the union leaders agreed to dampen pay rises in return for regular reductions in income tax rates. They also negotiated a new system of pay determination for public service employees under the rubric of "benchmarking" using external assessment of pay scales for assorted grades.

The era of [Christian democratic](/wiki/Christian_democrat "Christian democrat") style corporatism also saw a dramatic fall in trade union density from 62% in 1980 to 31% in 2007 and consolidation through mergers of many affiliated trade unions.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Efforts to launch recruitment and organising initiatives failed to secure adequate support from affiliated unions while attempts to secure indirect forms of union recognition through legislation collapsed after successful legal challenges and appeals by the anti-union [Ryanair](/wiki/Ryanair "Ryanair") company.

Ireland's period of centralised 'social pacts' ended in late 2009 when the government imposed pay cuts of between 5% and 8% on public service employees. The joint-stewardship of the state's [FÁS](/wiki/F%C3%81S "FÁS") training and employment authority by Congress and IBEC and accompanied waste of public and EU funds and excessive spending on directors 'junkets' further weakened the public standing of Congress and its 'social partnership' structures.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In an assessment of the post-partnership situation, Congress general secretary David Begg prepared a strategic review paper in which he identified the increasing weakness of the Congress and individual trade unions being due to "recession and change in the balance of power with capital" as well as job cuts, poor organisation, especially in high-technology companies, and a growing rift between public and private sector employees.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[5]](#cite_note-5)

On a more positive note Begg asserted that the ending of social partnership arrangements "liberates us to advocate and campaign for our own policies".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Other activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other activities")]

A "mass rally", organised by the Irish Congress of Trade Unions, [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"), and the [Rainbow Project](/wiki/Rainbow_Project "Rainbow Project") in support of [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") in Northern Ireland[[7]](#cite_note-Pressure-7) took place on 13 June 2015 in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), with a 20,000 person turnout.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## General Secretaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: General Secretaries")]

:   1959: [James Larkin Jnr](/wiki/James_Larkin_Jnr "James Larkin Jnr")[[19]](#cite_note-constitution-19)
:   1960: [Leo Crawford](/wiki/Leo_Crawford "Leo Crawford") and [Ruaidhri Roberts](/wiki/Ruaidhri_Roberts "Ruaidhri Roberts")[[19]](#cite_note-constitution-19)
:   1967: [Ruaidhri Roberts](/wiki/Ruaidhri_Roberts "Ruaidhri Roberts")[[19]](#cite_note-constitution-19)
:   1982: [Donal Nevin](/wiki/Donal_Nevin "Donal Nevin")[[19]](#cite_note-constitution-19)
:   1989: [Peter Cassells](/wiki/Peter_Cassells "Peter Cassells")[[19]](#cite_note-constitution-19)
:   2001: [David Begg](/wiki/David_Begg_(trade_unionist) "David Begg (trade unionist)")[[19]](#cite_note-constitution-19)
:   2015: [Patricia King](/wiki/Patricia_King_(trade_unionist) "Patricia King (trade unionist)")[[19]](#cite_note-constitution-19)
:   2022: [Owen Reidy](/wiki/Owen_Reidy "Owen Reidy")[[20]](#cite_note-20)
